coredata.dc:description: Nanofiltration membranes are extensively implemented in water treatment, particularly for the efficient interception of toxic heavy metal ions. This work introduces a novel functional membrane fabricated via interfacial polymerization, incorporating nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) as additives to enhance mercury removal from aqueous solutions. The integration of carbon dots has been demonstrated to significantly augment the membrane’s hydrophilicity, thereby improving the pure water flux. Moreover, it modulates the crosslinking degree of the polyamide layer, enhancing mercury retention. The N, S-CDs, due to their high selectivity for Hg (II), confer the modified membrane with fluorescence detection capabilities, allowing for the precise quantification of mercury rejection. The membrane achieved a remarkable Hg (II) retention rate of 94.85 %, with a pure water flux of 10.68 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, and attained a detection limit of 5.49 × 10−7 M for Hg (II). The durability and reusability of the membrane are substantiated by soaking and recycling trials, underscoring the stable encapsulation of N, S-CDs within the nanofiltration matrix.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofiltration membranes are extensively implemented in water treatment, particularly for the efficient interception of toxic heavy metal ions. This work introduces a novel functional membrane fabricated via interfacial polymerization, incorporating nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) as additives to enhance mercury removal from aqueous solutions. The integration of carbon dots has been demonstrated to significantly augment the membrane’s hydrophilicity, thereby improving the pure water flux. Moreover, it modulates the crosslinking degree of the polyamide layer, enhancing mercury retention. The N, S-CDs, due to their high selectivity for Hg (II), confer the modified membrane with fluorescence detection capabilities, allowing for the precise quantification of mercury rejection. The membrane achieved a remarkable Hg (II) retention rate of 94.85 %, with a pure water flux of 10.68 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, and attained a detection limit of 5.49 × 10−7 M for Hg (II). The durability and reusability of the membrane are substantiated by soaking and recycling trials, underscoring the stable encapsulation of N, S-CDs within the nanofiltration matrix.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Water quality constitutes an essential resource for the sustenance and progress of human civilization, exerting a profound influence on the equilibrium of societal and economic expansion [1]. In recent years, the discord between environmental degradation and the imperative of sustainable economic progress has emerged as a salient challenge [2]. Among the myriad of environmental contaminants, heavy metal ion pollution in water stands out as a particularly exigent issue, with mercury emerging as a predominant culprit due to its remarkable bioenrichment and pernicious effects on human health and the environment [3,4]. Mercury pollution inflicts substantial detriment to our ecological systems and poses a grave risk to human health, notably inflicting damage upon the nervous system, cerebral functions, renal health, and the endocrine balance [5,6]. Consequently, addressing the challenges associated with the detection and removal of mercury from aquatic environments is imperative. The development of efficient and accurate methods for mercury detection and its subsequent elimination from water sources is essential to mitigate the associated environmental and health risks [7].

Nanofiltration technology represents an advanced pressure-driven membrane separation process, which capitalizes on the inherent physical sieving characteristics of membrane to effect selective separation of metal ions [8]. This technology is thus regarded as a highly effective method for water purification. The structure of nanofiltration membranes is typically multi-layered, consisting of a non-woven support layer and a functional layer. Polyamide membranes, synthesized through the interfacial polymerization of amine and acyl chloride monomers on a porous substrate, have become an immense potential due to their exceptional performance in the field of metal ion separation [9,10]. The functional layer of the nanofiltration membrane is pivotal in imparting the membrane with remarkable water permeability and selective separation efficacy. The pore size, surface hydrophilicity, and roughness of the membrane are critical parameters that can be significantly altered by various modification strategies [11,12]. These factors are particularly crucial in determining the membrane’s ability to effectively separate metal ions, thereby influencing the overall efficiency and applicability of the nanofiltration process in water treatment applications. Among the diverse membrane modification strategies, the integration of innovative nanostructures is noticeable as a particularly efficacious method to bolster membrane performance [13,14]. Such enhancements are realized through the refinement of key attributes, including the membrane’s surface charge properties, pore size, wettability and so on. These strategic alterations result in a marked enhancement in the membrane’s ability to effectively remove or separate targeted pollutants [15]. As a result, the comprehensive efficacy of the modified membranes is significantly amplified, paving the way for more efficient and targeted filtration processes.

Carbon dots (CDs) represent an emerging class of nanostructured fluorescent materials (<10 nm) that exhibit immense promise for utilization in the field of sensor technology, particularly for fluorescence-based detection for kinds of metal ions [16]. Their appeal is largely attributed to the minimal toxicity, superior biocompatibility, high fluorescence quantum yield, robust fluorescence stability and tunable excitation and emission spectra [17]. Moreover, the surface of these luminescent carbon nanoparticles is endowed with plenty of hydrophilic functional groups, which, in conjunction with their inherent chemical stability and non-toxic nature, facilitates seamless integration into organic frameworks, including membrane structures [18]. The strategic modification of CDs nanoparticles of their incorporation into the polyamide layer of a membrane yields a multiplicity of benefits. Such modifications not only augment the hydrophilic nature of the membrane surface but also modulate the surface topography by increasing its roughness and the complexity of the polyamide layer [19]. These enhancements are instrumental in improving the membrane’s overall performance, particularly in the interaction between membrane surface and the target analytes.

In this work, a nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) were incorporated into a piperazine (PIP) aqueous solution, serving as a hydrophilic additive. This mixture was then subjected to an interfacial polymerization reaction with trimesoyl chloride (TMC) in an oil phase on the surface of a polyethersulfone (PES) ultrafiltration membrane, resulting in the formation of a CDs-modified polyamide thin layer. The incorporation of CDs into the composite membrane led to a significant fluorescence quenching effect in the presence of Hg (II) ions. When compared to the polyether sulfone nanocomposite membrane embedded with UiO-66-SO3H nanoparticles, which demonstrated a Hg (II) retention rate of 87.7 % and a high permeability of 9.57 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 [20], the introduction of N, S-CDs into the polyamide nanofilter membrane resulted in the Hg (II) retention rate as high as 94.85 %. In addition, the water flux was increased from 8.41 to 10.68 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, and the fluorescence intensity was reduced by 73.4 %, highlighting its potential as an advanced material for environmental applications and providing a new method for simultaneous removal and detection of mercury.

Polyether sulfone (PES, 50 kDa) ultrafiltration membrane was used as the substrate for the fabrication of the composite membrane, which was provided by ANDER Membrane Co., LTD. (Beijing, China). Piperazine (PIP, 99 %), o-phenylenediamine (OPD, 98 %), p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA, 99 %), triethyl phosphonoacetate (TEPA), and calcium chloride (97 %) were purchased from McLean Reagent (Shanghai, China). n-hexane (97 %), tribenzoyl chloride (TMC, 98 %), potassium hydroxide, 3-mertopropionic acid (3-MPA, 98 %) were acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD.

A facile one-step solvothermal synthesis method was employed to prepare the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) (Scheme 1 ) [21]. Initially, OPD (0.73 g, 6.0 mmol), PABA (0.35 g, 2.5 mmol), and 3-MPA (0.2 g, 2.0 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of DMF, followed by the addition of H2O2 (2.0 mL). The mixture was then subjected to ultrasonication for 20 min to ensure thorough mixing. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and maintained at 180 °C for a period of 8 h to facilitate the formation of N, S-CDs. Upon completion of the solvothermal synthesis, the reaction mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature. The resulting N, S-CDs were then isolated by centrifugation at 10,000 rmp for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove any impurities by filtration. To further purify the N, S-CDs, a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 500 Da was employed. The dialysis process was carried out for a duration of 24 h to ensure the removal of any residual small molecules that may affect the quality and properties of the N, S-CDs. Following the dialysis, the purified N, S-CDs were obtained by a gentle drying process to yield a fine powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optimization of nanofiltration membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, it is of importance to determine the optimal monomer concentration for the fabrication of N, S-CDs encapsulated polyamide (N, S-CDs@PA) nanofiltration membranes. Utilizing the interfacial polymerization technique, a series of nine membranes were synthesized with varying monomer concentrations: TMC (0.1, 0.2, 0.3 wt%) and PIP (0.05, 0.15, 0.25 wt%). The experimental procedure commenced with the preparation of the PES ultrafiltration base membrane, which was mounted in a custom device to ensure the PES layer was in contact with the solution. A 0.01 wt% N, S-CDs solution was then introduced to the PIP solution with the aforementioned concentration gradient, allowing the base membrane to be immersed in the mixture for a period of 5 min. After immersion, the solution was decanted and the membrane was air-dried. Following this, the PES substrate enriched with N, S-CDs was immersed in a TMC/n-hexane solution (0.1, 0.2, 0.3 wt%) for 2 min to induce the formation of a polyamide (PA) separation layer. The solution was subsequently decanted and the membrane was air-dried once more. The final step involved placing the resultant membrane in a well-ventilated area to facilitate the completion of the crosslinking process.

To assess the influence of N, S-CDs on the performance of PES-PA membranes, 38 mg of N, S-CDs was thoroughly diluted in 30 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min to ensure homogeneity and then filtered using a vacuum pump to eliminate any insoluble components. Thereafter, distinct volumes (2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 mL) of the N, S-CDs solution were incrementally added to the aqueous phase containing the optimized monomer concentration, thus establishing a gradient of N, S-CDs concentrations within the aqueous phase. Employing the aforementioned methodology and the identified optimal monomer concentrations, the N, S-CDs@PA nanofiltration membrane was successfully synthesized on a PES ultrafiltration substrate.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Tensor 37, Bruker, Germany) in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 was recorded to identify the chemical composition of the N, S-CDs and the functional groups for the membrane. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI, America) was utilized to investigate the elemental composition of the membrane and to monitor the chemical environmental variation in functional groups before and after exposure to mercury ions. The surface morphology of the membranes was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, D-457, USA), revealing the structural characteristics and any modifications induced by the incorporation of N, S-CDs. To assess the hydrophilicity of the membrane, contact angle measurements were performed using a contact angle instrument (JY-82B, Kruss, DSA), offering insights into the wettability and surface properties of the modified membranes. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Dimension ICON, Bruker, Germany) was used to further investigate the surface morphology and roughness of the samples. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were analysed using a T6 New Century UV–VIS spectrophotometer (AFS-933, China) and the fluorescence characteristics of the samples were confirmed using a Lengguang luminescence spectrophotometer F97PRO (China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing measurements for the membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence characteristics of the pristine PES membrane and the PES-PA membrane with/without incorporation of CDs were meticulously evaluated. To avoid the influence of fluorescence emission originating from the underlying film, a 420 nm filter was chosen for the fluorescence performance evaluation of the solid films. In details, a stock solution of mercury ions with a concentration of 10 mg/L was prepared by extracting 0.1 mL of a mercury standard solution and subsequently diluting it to a final volume of 10 mL using ultrapure deionized water. The stock solution was then utilized to create a series of mercury ion gradients by dispensing varying volumes (0.1 to 1.0 mL) into separate aliquots, each of which was diluted to a final volume of 10 mL to achieve distinct Hg (II) concentrations. The PES and PES-PA membranes were systematically immersed in these mercury ion solutions. Following a standardized period of immersion to allow for mercury ion adsorption, the fluorescence intensity of each membrane was quantified using a fluorescence spectrometer. This process was repeated for each incrementally diluted mercury solution to establish a comprehensive dataset that correlates the fluorescence response with the concentration of Hg (II) ions.

In this study, a custom-designed terminal filtration apparatus, characterized by a volume capacity of 12 mL, a membrane surface area of 4.52 cm2, and capable of withstanding a maximum pressure of 40 bar, was employed to evaluate the permeability and repulsion properties of the membrane. To ensure the consistency and reliability of the experimental outcomes, each membrane was subjected to a preconditioning phase, involving the compaction of the membrane at 25 °C using deionized water at a pressure of 20 bar for a minimum duration of 5 min, thereby attaining a steady-state condition that is essential for accurate performance assessment. The permeation flux (L m−2 h−1) was quantified using the following formula (1):
(1)
F l u x =
V p
t × A
where Vp (L) denotes the volume of the penetrant, A (m2) represents the effective membrane area, and t (h) is the duration of the filtration process. The pure water permeability (PWP, L m−2 h−1 bar−1) was derived by considering the transmembrane pressure (TMP, bar) as formula (2):
(2)
P W P =
F l u x
T M P

Furthermore, the retention rate of the test solution was calculated using the following relationship (3) [22]:
(3)
R = ( 1 -
C p
0.5 × (
C f

)
) × 100 %
where C p (mg/L), C f (mg/L), and C r (mg/L), correspond to the concentrations of penetrant, feed, and retention in the solution, respectively. The concentrations were accurately determined using an atomic fluorescence spectrometer (AFS-8220, Titan Instruments, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of the N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectroscopy analysis elucidated the chemical composition of N, S-CDs. An absorption band observed in the spectrum ranging from 3211 to 3321 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl group (OH), indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups [23]. Furthermore, a pronounced absorption peak at 1657 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO bonds in acylamino groups, which points to the incorporation of carboxyl group within the N, S-CDs structure [24]. The absorption peaks at 1494 cm−1 and 1222 cm−1 are indicative of the CC stretching vibration. The collective presence of hydroxyl, and carbonyl group on the surface of N, S-CDs is instrumental in endowing these nanomaterials with exceptional water solubility [25].

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectral characteristics of N, S-CDs are depicted in Fig. 1 b. The UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibits a broad absorbance band centered at 422 nm with a shoulder peak at 386 nm. This prominent peak is ascribed to the n-π* transition of the carbonyl (CO) or amino (NH) groups within the N, S-CDs structure [26]. In the fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs, the maximum excitation wavelength is identified at 419 nm (green line in Fig. 1b) and the corresponding maximum emission wavelength is observed at 567 nm (yellow line in Fig. 1b).

To assess the potential of N, S-CDs as a fluorescent probe for the detection of mercury (II) ions, the selectivity of the N, S-CDs towards various metal cations is investigated. For this purpose, the selectivity was evaluated by adding solutions containing a range of metal cations, including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+ and Hg2+, each at a concentration of 100 mg/L, to the N, S-CDs solution. The fluorescence intensity was then measured at the excitation wavelength of 419 nm to determine the response of the N, S-CDs to the presence of these metal ions (Fig. 1c). It can be concluded that the presence of Hg (II) ions induces a pronounced fluorescence quenching effect on N, S-CDs, characterized by a significant reduction in fluorescence intensity with a decrement of 73.4 %. In contrast, the influence of other metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs is found to be negligible. This distinctive selectivity of N, S-CDs for Hg (II) is further underscored by the minimal interference observed from the presence of other metal ions during the quenching process through competitive fluorescence titration. A series of ionic solutions containing different metal ions at a concentration of 100 mg/L were incrementally introduced into the N, S-CDs dilution. It is found that, with the exception of Hg2+, the fluorescence of the N, S-CDs remained unaffected by the presence of other metal ions, thereby highlighting the high selectivity and sensitivity of N, S-CDs towards Hg2+ in aqueous media (Fig. 1d).

Moreover, Fig. 1e depicts a progressive decline in the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs with an increasing concentration of Hg (II) ions, ranging from 0 to 10 μM. Based on this, Fig. 1f illustrates a linear proportionality between the relative fluorescence change (I 0/I) of the N, S-CDs and the concentration of Hg2+ ions spanning 0 to 10 μM in water. Capitalizing on this pronounced linear response to Hg2+ ions, the limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ was determined to be as low as 8.0 × 10−8 M. The limit of detection (LOD) for N, S-CDs in relation to Hg (II) is determined using the formula 3σ/K, where σ represents the standard deviation of ten parallel blank samples and K denotes the slope of the calibration curve [27]. Additionally, the visual representation (inset in Fig. 1f) confirms that N, S-CDs exhibit a yellow fluorescence under ultraviolet irradiation at 365 nm, which is obviously quenched upon the addition of Hg2+. Fig. 1g presents a TEM image of the N, S-CDs, revealing their monodisperse, spherical nature and uniform dispersion without significant agglomeration. A statistical analysis of 128 carbon dots from the TEM image indicates an average particle size of 2.63 ± 0.08 nm for this sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of N, S-CDs@PA membrane
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of the three membranes (PES ultrafiltration base membrane, PES-PA nanofiltration membrane and N, S-CDs@PA membrane) over the range of 500–4000 cm−1 were recorded in Fig. 2 a to identify the functional groups in the samples. The absorption peaks observed at 1483 cm−1 and 1578 cm−1 are assignable to the CC double bonds present within the benzene rings of the polymeric matrix for PES membrane [28]. Additionally, the peaks detected at 1289 cm−1 and 1318 cm−1 are indicative of the SO double bonds [29]. The persistence of these two absorption peaks throughout the subsequent modifications for PES-PA nanofiltration membrane and N, S-CDs@PA membrane confirms that the incorporation of an amide layer and CDs does not alter the fundamental structure of the PES membrane matrix. Moreover, a novel absorption peak emerged in the PES-PA membrane at 1631 cm−1, which corresponds to the CO stretching vibration, substantiating the successful formation of an amide bond during the interfacial polymerization [30]. Concurrently, the preservation of this peak in the N, S-CDs@PA composite suggests that the introduction of CDs in the aqueous phase does not disrupt the formation or stability of the nanofiltration layer.

An investigation into the point of zero charge (PZC) was undertaken to delineate the surface characteristics of N, S-CDs@PA membrane across a broad pH range of 2–11. The detection commenced with the preparation of a 10 mL aliquot of 0.01 mol/L NaNO3 solution, which was then transferred to a shaking tube. The pH was adjusted across the range of 2–11 using dilute NaOH and HNO3 solutions. Subsequently, 0.05 g of fragmented N, S-CDs@PA membrane was introduced into the solution. The mixture was subjected to constant agitation at 25 °C in a shaking incubator for a duration of 72 h. Post incubation, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to obtain a clear filtrate, and the pH of this filtrate was measured. As depicted in Fig. 2b, the PZC is identified at the intersection of two curves, which corresponds to a pH of 6.5. At this pH, the membrane surface is electrically neutral, bearing no net positive or negative charge. Within the pH range of 2–6.5, where the pH is below the PZC, the positive charge density on the N, S-CDs@PA membrane surface escalates. This increase in positive charge endows the membrane with a heightened repellency for cationic species [31,32]. Given that mercury ions (Hg2+) are predominantly soluble in acidic media, the N, S-CDs@PA membrane exhibits a propensity for the sequestration of Hg2+ from aqueous solutions.

Fig. 2(c) clearly demonstrates that the PES ultrafiltration base membrane possesses a uniformly smooth and flat surface topography. In contrast, as illustrated in Fig. 2d, the surface of the PES-PA membrane is distinguished by the presence of granular convex structures, which contribute to a notable increase in surface roughness relative to the PES base membrane. This observation is indicative of the successful interfacial polymerization on the polyether sulfone substrate, leading to the formation of a polyamide active layer [33]. Upon examining the CDs-modified nanofiltration membrane, the N, S-CDs@PA membrane (Fig. 2e), it is observed that the membrane surface also exhibits a uniform distribution of granular protrusions. This morphology is a characteristic outcome of the interfacial polymerization process, a phenomenon well-documented in prior literature [34,35]. The structural transformation on the base film’s surface is attributed to the deposition of the functional layer. Concurrently, these protrusions augment the specific surface area of the membrane, altering its surface roughness and enhancing the permeability of the nanofiltration membrane [36]. However, when compared to Fig. 2d, the N, S-CDs@PA membrane surface appears to be more refined. This refinement may be attributed to the impact of CDs on the interfacial polymerization process, potentially slowing the reaction rate and culminating in a more compact and uniform separation layer [37].

XPS analysis was employed to investigate the chemical environmental changes during the progressive surface modification of the PES membrane (Fig. 3 ). It can be concluded that the PES ultrafiltration membranes exhibit distinct binding energy (BE) peaks corresponding to O1s, C1s, and S2p characteristics (Fig. 3a), while the weak N1s BE peaks are likely attributed to stabilizers or trace impurities. In the PS-PA membrane prepared by interfacial polymerization, the absence of the S2p peak suggests that the polyamide separation layer formed through interfacial polymerization effectively covers the upper surface of the PES membrane, rendering the S2p peak undetectable by XPS. In the N, S-CDs@PA membrane following carbon dot modification, the re-emergence of a low-intensity S2p peak indicates the successful incorporation of −SH groups from the N, S-CDs into the polyamide nanofilter membrane, where they are distributed within the surface pore structure after modification. Additionally, the progressive increase in N1s signal strength correlates with the modification process, further confirming the polyamide layer’s coverage. In Fig. 3b, the BE peaks observed at 164.67 eV and 164.22 eV in Fig. 3b are assignable to the S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 transitions of C-S-C, respectively, while peaks at 168.86 eV and 167.66 eV are indicative of the SO2− structure [38,39]. In contrast, the high-resolution XPS scan of the N, S-CDs@PA membrane reveals significant alterations that the −SH peak is attenuated and the SO2− structure is even more diminished, which may be attributed to the challenging detection of sulfur elements in the PES base film due to the overlay of the CDs-modified polyamide layer. In this separation layer, sulfur predominantly exists as thiol groups [40]. Nitrogen appears in a simpler form, exhibiting a comparable structure as shown in Fig. 3c. Upon deconvolution of the O1s BE peak, a more intricate cleavage pattern is observed in the PES base membrane that is attributed to the presence of SO bonds (531.64 eV) [41]. During subsequent modifications, the detectable SO2− structure diminishes and the fine structure at 533.43 eV gradually vanishes, a consequence of the overlay by new separation layers, which is also in accordance with the results of analysis for S element [42] (see Fig. 3d).

To further understand the surface characteristics of the membranes, atomic force microscopy (AFM) was employed to examine the prepared samples (Fig. 4 a-c and Fig. 4a1-c1). The AFM findings corroborate the SEM observations, revealing an increase in surface roughness of the PES ultrafiltration base membrane subsequent to the introduction of a polyamide layer with R a increased from 4.41 nm to 98.8 nm. However, the incorporation of N, S-CDs within an aqueous solution during the casting process, which become embedded on the surface of the hybrid membrane, leads to a reduction in surface roughness with R a decreased from 98.8 nm to 46.2 nm. As depicted in Fig. 4a2, 4b2, and 4c2, the introduction of N, S-CDs during the fabrication of the polyamide membrane precipitates a marked decrease in the water contact angle (70.4° for PES, 62.7° for PES-PA and 20.5° for N, S-CDs@PA). This phenomenon suggests that N, S-CDs, when used as an additive in the aqueous phase, can significantly enhance the physical attributes of the nanofiltration membrane surfaces, thereby augmenting their hydrophilicity. During the interfacial polymerization process, N, S-CDs are uniformly integrated into both the surface and the internal pore surfaces of the nanofiltration membrane. The homogeneous distribution of these hydrophilic functional groups at the interfacial regions of the separation layers is posited to enhance the pure water flux across the membrane, which is beneficial for the application in water treatment. The enhanced hydrophilicity is attributed to the large specific surface area and superior dispersion of N, S-CDs that bear a multitude of hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence factors on water flux and retention rate for the membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 a delineates the variations in pure water permeability and the mercury acetate retention rate for nanofiltration membranes modified with varying concentrations of N, S-CDs. The N, S-CDs were dispersed in ultra-pure water to generate a gradient of N, S-CDs concentrations within the aqueous solutions. Concurrently, the concentration of piperazine (PIP) in the aqueous phase was uniformly maintained at 0.15 wt%. During the interfacial polymerization process, the aqueous phase was allowed to react for 5 min, while the organic phase reaction was set for 2 min. Trimesoyl chloride (TMC) was employed as the monomer at a concentration of 0.3 wt% in organic phase, with n-hexane serving as the solvent. This methodology was employed to elucidate the influence of N, S-CDs concentration on the membrane properties. As depicted in Fig. 5a, an increment in N, S-CDs concentration to 0.005 wt% resulted in a marked increase in pure water flux from 8.41 to 10.68 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, accompanied by a reduction in the mercury acetate retention rate from 96.87 % to 94.85 %. Beyond this concentration threshold, the water flux exhibited a pronounced decline, plummeting to 2.78 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. The initial increase in water flux at low N, S-CDs concentrations is hypothesized to be due to the hydrophilic nature of N, S-CDs, which enhances the hydrophilicity of the membrane [44]. The nanoscale size and uniform dispersion of N, S-CDs are believed to create water channels with diminished molecular transfer resistance, thus augmenting the membrane’s permeability [45]. However, as the concentration of N, S-CDs escalates from 0.005 to 0.025 wt%, an overabundance of N, S-CDs may progressively impede the interfacial polymerization, leading to a decreased reaction rate and the formation of a more compact polyamide layer [46]. This alteration is conjectured to attenuate the water flux of the membrane, a finding that corroborates the increased membrane roughness observed via AFM analysis. Furthermore, the data presented in the figure indicates that as the concentration of N, S-CDs increases, the contact angle progressively decreases, signifying an enhancement in hydrophilicity. This observation allows us to infer a correlation between the hydrophilicity of the membrane and the quantity of N, S-CDs incorporated. However, it is important to note that there is no conclusive evidence to establish a direct relationship between the hydrophilicity of the membrane and its overall performance properties.

The extent of interfacial polymerization significantly influences the separation characteristics of nanofiltration membranes. Fig. 5b illustrates the impact of the aqueous phase treatment duration on the membrane properties during the interfacial polymerization process. An elongation of the treatment time results in a gradual decline in pure water flux and a concurrent increase in the retention rate of Hg2+, indicative of a trade-off phenomenon [47]. This trend is attributed to the thorough infiltration of the aqueous solution into the substrate membrane’s interstitial spaces, allowing for extensive coverage of the membrane surface and pore structure by PIP [48]. Such coverage is essential for enhancing the subsequent reaction efficiency with TMC, leading to a higher degree of cross-linking and increased polyamide density within the membrane matrix. Consequently, this results in a downward trend in the pure water flux from 19.71 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 to 10.68 L m−2 h−1 bar−1. Ultimately, once the substrate membrane reaches saturation with aqueous phase PIP adsorption (in approximately 3.5 min), it enters a relatively stable state, beyond which the retention rate cannot be further enhanced (from 83.11 % to 94.85 %). The stabilized pure water flux for the nanofiltration membrane was recorded as 10.68 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 with a retention rate of 94.85 % for Hg2+. Fig. 5c presents the analogous effect of the organic phase treatment duration on membrane properties. As the organic phase treatment time is extended (from 0.5 to 5 min), the cross-linking degree of the polyamide layer intensifies with the progression of the reaction, culminating in a reduced water flux (from 13.55 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 to 5.02 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) and an elevated retention rate (from 90.61 % to 98.91 %) for the nanofiltration membrane. In summary, the optimal reagent ratios for fabricating nanofiltration membranes with superior retention capabilities are as follows: N, S-CDs at 0.005 wt%, PIP at 0.15 wt%, and TMC at 0.3 wt%. In addition, the ideal preparation conditions involve an aqueous phase treatment of 5 min and an organic phase treatment of 2 min. Under these conditions, the retention rate reaches up to 94.85 % for 1000 mg/L mercury acetate solution with pure water flux of 10.68 L m−2 h−1 bar−1.

In addition, the duration of post-reaction aging significantly influences the interfacial polymerization process and consequently the fabrication of the resultant membrane. Following the initial polymerization, membranes were subjected to thermal aging treatment at 30, 40, 50, 60, and 70 °C for 60 min. Subsequently, the mercury retention rate and the pure water flux of the membranes were evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, an increase in oven temperature correlates with a decrease in the pure water flux (from 10.68 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 to 8.12 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) and a slight elevation in the Hg2+ retention (from 94.85 % to 96.22 %). Notably, this trend stabilizes at 50 °C, with the retention rate plateauing at about 96.11 %. This stabilization suggests that post-initial interfacial polymerization, the polyamide layer undergoes further cross-linking upon thermal treatment at a specific temperature, thereby impacting the membrane’s performance on metal retention. Moreover, selectivity is a pivotal metric for assessing the practicality and longevity of a membrane. The competitive separation capabilities of the N, S-CDs@PA membrane were probed through a series of experiments involving metal ions as depicted in Fig. 5e. The addition of Na2SO4, NaCl, MgSO4, and MgCl2 to Hg2+ solutions was followed by mercury retention tests under varying anionic and cationic interferences. The findings indicate a marginal reduction in both the pure water flux and the Hg2+ retention of the N, S-CDs@PA membranes, with different cations inducing a comparable decrement in the mercury retention rate (91.22 % for Na2SO4, 91.23 % for NaCl, 90.87 % for MgSO4 and 91.54 % for MgCl2). This uniformity suggests that size exclusion is the predominant separation mechanism of the membrane for mercury removal.

Furthermore, the impact of pH on the membrane’s separation efficiency was examined across a range of 2–11. Each membrane sample was immersed in solutions with varying pH values for 24 h, after which they were rinsed with ultra-pure water to ascertain the pure water flux and retention (Fig. 5f). A significant increase in pure water flux and a marked decrease in Hg2+ retention are observed when the pH exceeds 8. In contrast, the membrane maintains its functionality within a pH range of 2–8, with minimal fluctuations in both pure water flux (10.62 L m−2 h−1 bar−1) and retention rate (94.88 %). This pH sensitivity may be attributed to the alkaline-induced degradation of the amide structure [49]. Typically, heavy metals in water undergo alkali treatment during the pre-treatment phase, and most membrane treatment units operate in a neutral or mildly acidic to mildly alkaline environment, thus ensuring the membrane’s substantial stability under operational conditions.

Fig. 6 illustrates the impact of modification of PES-based membranes on the retention performance for diverse metal salts. The comparative analysis reveals that the interfacial polymerized polyamide membranes substantially enhance the retention rates of Hg2+ by a factor of 10.38, Na2SO4 by 10.14, and MgSO4 by 8.16, when juxtapose with PES-based membranes. Notably, even the retention rates for NaCl and MgCl2 are elevated by more than a single-fold increment (for NaCl by 2.13 and for MgCl2 by 2.59). This enhancement is considered to stem from the membrane’s surface charge properties, which exhibit a superior permeability induction for divalent magnesium ions compared to monovalent sodium ions, thereby conferring a higher retention rate for sodium salts. Given that mercury ions possess the same charge as magnesium ions but exhibit a significantly larger ionic radius, the N, S-CDs@PA membrane demonstrates enhanced retention efficacy for mercury ions. Specifically, the retention rate for Hg2+ peaks at an impressive 94.39 %, followed by Na2SO4 at 84.3 %, MgSO4 at 49.45 %, MgCl2 at 19.77 %, and NaCl at 17.77 %. It is noteworthy that the incorporation of 0.005 wt% N, S-CDs into the PES-PA membrane does not yield a significant alteration in the retention efficacy for the metal ion species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of the membrane towards Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: As the CDs-based fluorescent probe sensitive to Hg2+ ions are incorporated during the polymerization process, the nanofiltration membrane was thereby endowed with both interception capability and a novel detective function, culminating in the fabrication of a multifunctional membrane. Fig. 7 a illustrates the surface fluorescence emission of the three distinct membranes. The PES membrane exhibits a fluorescence emission peak centered at 587 nm, while the polyamide coating introduced via interfacial polymerization not only enhances the fluorescence intensity of the modified membrane but also induces a redshift of the emission peak to 642 nm. By contrast, the incorporation of carbon dots significantly amplifies the membrane’s fluorescence intensity that the fluorescence emission peak is recorded at 567 nm (λ ex = 419 nm) utilizing a 420 nm filter, aligning with the intrinsic fluorescence characteristics of the N, S-CDs, thereby corroborating the successful encapsulation of the N, S-CDs within the membrane. Moreover, the inset in Fig. 7a depicts the fluorescence variation of the membrane upon exposure to mercury solutions of varying concentrations. A gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed with increasing mercury concentration from 0 to 10 μM, manifesting a pronounced quenching effect. At a mercury concentration of 5 mg/L, the membrane’s fluorescence intensity stabilized. Fig. 7b delineates the linear relationship between the change in membrane fluorescence intensity (I 0/I) and Hg2+ ion concentration, demonstrating a strong correlation within the range of 4–10 μM, which enabled the calculation of a detection limit (LOD) for Hg2+ as low as 5.49 × 10−7 M (3σ/S). The uniform distribution and stability of CDs within the film greatly affect its fluorescent characteristics and the precision of detection capabilities. The distribution of N, S-CDs on the composite membrane’s surface is investigated through fluorescence variation rates calculated as (I 0-I)/I 0 at various positions in Fig. 7c. The minimal variance in fluorescence emission across different membrane positions, approximately 0.15 %, indicates a uniform encapsulation of N, S-CDs. Besides, the inset photographs display the fluorescence emission under ambient sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet illumination, which also confirm the distinct and uniform distribution of the CDs. In addition, the uniformity and stability of the CDs dispersion within the film substantially influence the fluorescence characteristics and the fidelity of detection. To assess the stability of the encapsulated CDs, nanofiltration membranes were immersed in deionized water for intervals from 1 h to 5 d. After the immersion, 2 mL of water samples were extracted and their fluorescence emission was measured (Fig. 7d). The negligible change in fluorescence intensity across varying soak times suggests that the encapsulation of N, S-CDs within the PA film is stable and does not degrade over time. This stable encapsulation is crucial for ensuring the accuracy of mercury ion detection in subsequent nanofiltration membrane applications. Furthermore, selectivity is also pivotal for accurate metal ion detection. To evaluate this, a membrane of identical dimensions was immersed in 16 different ionic solutions, each with a concentration of 100 mg/L, with a control group set for comparison (Fig. 7e). It can be concluded that only the film soaked in Hg2+ solution exhibits stable fluorescence quenching, indicating a significant detection effect compared to films soaked in other ionic solutions. Fig. 7f presents the pure water flux and retention rate of Hg2+ in 8 consecutive cycles of application using a custom terminal filtration setup, in which the N, S-CDs@PA membrane, pre-compressed to a stable state with deionized water under a specific pressure of 5.6 bar. The pure water flux remains consistent around 10.68 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and the retention rate maintains at about 94.85 %, respectively, demonstrating good practical stability without performance degradation upon repeated use. Furthermore, a comparative analysis (Table S1) of the properties of various membrane materials has been compiled and is detailed in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction mechanism of the membrane towards Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of mercury ion interception and detection was elucidated by XPS analysis of membrane samples before and after mercury interception. As depicted in Fig. 8 a, the XPS survey of the N, S-CDs@PA sample post mercury ion interception not only exhibits the characteristic peaks of O1s, N1s, and C1s but also prominently displays the BE peaks of Hg4f at 104.56 eV and 100.49 eV, thereby confirming the successful capture of mercury ions [50]. Additionally, in the high-resolution scan of S element (Fig. 8b), the disappearance of the S2p peak at 164.24 eV, which is characteristic of sulfhydryl sulfur, post-mercury retention and fluorescence detection by the N, S-CDs@PA membrane, suggests that the quenching of the CDs is predicated on the strong interaction between sulfur and mercury [51,52]. Furthermore, the BE peak of SO2− in the N, S-CDs@PA membrane, primarily originating from the PES base membrane, shifts from 168.5 eV to 168.47 eV, indicating that the oxygen atoms connected to sulfur are a significant factor in mercury trapping [53,54]. Concurrently, in the N1s XPS spectrum depicted in Fig. 8c, the BE peak associated with NH shifts from 401.39 eV to 401.69 eV upon interaction with mercury, while the BE peak corresponding to NCO transitions from 399.90 eV to 399.95 eV [55]. Similarly, in the O1s spectrum presented in Fig. 8d, the BE peaks for OH, CO, and CO, initially positioned at 532.62 eV, 531.37 eV, and 530.83 eV, respectively, are marginally displaced to 532.70 eV, 531.56 eV, and 530.90 eV [56,57]. This increase in binding energy is attributed to the formation of coordination bonds between Hg (II) and N/O atoms originated from N, S-CDs@PA, leading to a reduction in the electron cloud density around the N/O atoms [58,59].

In summary, the integration of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon dots into membrane systems represents a promising avenue for the development of high-performance separation and detection platforms. The integration of these CDs not only modifies the membrane’s surface topography but also significantly enhances its hydrophilicity, leading to an increased pure water flux. Furthermore, the encapsulation of CDs imparts the membrane with a unique capability for the detection of Hg (II) ions, capitalizing on their selective fluorescence response to Hg (II). Moreover, the membrane exhibited consistent performance in terms of pure water flux and Hg(II) retention rate across 8 consecutive reuses, indicative of excellent practical stability without degradation in performance. The synergistic interplay of CDs’ physicochemical properties and the adaptability of the membrane’s surface functionalities positions this technology as a potential candidate for applications in the field of fluorescence-based sensor technologies integrated with membranes.
